elements.sent-2411-001: The primary focus of this work is to investigate how the most emblematic classical probability density, namely a Gaussian, can be mapped to a valid quantum states.
elements.sent-2411-002: To explore this issue, we consider a Gaussian whose squared variance depends on a parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, depending on the value of $\lambda$, we study what happens in the classical-quantum correspondence as we change the indeterminacy of the classical particle.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, finding a correspondence between a classical state and a quantum state is not a trivial task.
elements.sent-2411-005: Quantum observables, described by Hermitian operators, do not generally commute, so a precise ordering must be introduced to resolve this ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we study two different arbitrary orderings: the first is an arbitrary ordering of the position and momentum observables; the second, which is the main focus of the present work, is an arbitrary ordering of the annihilation and creation operators.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this latter case, we find the interesting result that even a $\delta$-function, which in general has no quantum correspondence, can be mapped into a valid quantum state for a particular ordering, specifically the antinormal one (all creation operators are to the right of all annihilation operators in the product).
elements.sent-2411-008: This means that the Gaussian probability density corresponds to a valid quantum state, regardless of how localized classical particles are in phase space.
elements.sent-2411-009: There is no conceptual obstruction to go to higher arbitrary dimensions.) $\rho(q,p)$ which describes the probability density to find the particle with a certain position and velocity.
elements.sent-2411-010: Since the classical observables are functions on phase space, via the formula: \be \langle f\rangle_\rho=\int\de p \de q\, f(p,q) \rho(p,q) \ee the function $\rho$ can be seen as a map from the algebra of observables into the real numbers, whose physical meaning is the mean value of a measurement of the observable described by the real function $f$.
elements.sent-2411-011: A state therefore is a map from an algebra into numbers, which is the mathematical definition.
elements.sent-2411-012: Furthermore, we consider normalized states, i.e.\ $\int \de q \de p \, \rho(q,p)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-013: For the sake of brevity we skip important mathematical details, like the nature of the algebra and other properties of states.
elements.sent-2411-014: Consider a state which describes with some uncertainty a particle in a particular point in phase space, say the origin.
elements.sent-2411-015: A natural description is a Gaussian: \be \rho_\lambda=\frac\lambda\pi e^{-\lambda(p^2+q^2) \ee This is valid for any value of $\lambda$, and the functional is well defined in the limit $\lambda\to\infty$ for which we have the Dirac $\delta$ functional: \be \int\de p\de q\, \delta(p,q) f(p,q)=f(0,0) \ee which means that the particle is in the origin without any indetermination.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The generalization to an arbitrary location in phase space is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-017: A linear combination of states of the kind $\alpha\rho_1+(1-\alpha)\rho_2$, for $\alpha\in[0,1]$, called a convex sum, is still a state.
elements.sent-2411-018: Every state described by a function (not a functional) can be written in such a way.
elements.sent-2411-019: Pure states are those which cannot be written as a convex sum of two other states.
elements.sent-2411-020: Only the $\delta$ functionals are of this kind.
elements.sent-2411-021: In quantum mechanics the algebra of functions of $p$ and $q$ is substituted by the algebra of their noncommuting counterpart.
elements.sent-2411-022: Observables are self-adjoint operators, and a state is described by density matrix, a positive operator of trace one, whose eiegenvalues are all positive and smaller than one.
elements.sent-2411-023: Pure states are projectors, i.e.\ operators which can be written as $\hat\rho=\ket\psi\bra\psi$, for some vector in the Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is easy to see that the definition of pure states as states that cannot be written as convex sum of two other states, still holds.
elements.sent-2411-025: Weyl~\cite{Weyl,suleymanov2019wigner,Zampini:2005rx,tatarskiui1983wigner developed in the 1930s a map, called the Weyl map, with the purpose of mapping classical probability densities to quantum states.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The converse task has been extensively studied, with Wigner~\cite{Wigner being a pioneer in this field.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The Wigner function~\cite{Wigner provides a means to associate a quantum state with a classical probability density in phase space.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, only certain quantum states can be mapped to valid classical probability densities, and in fact, the majority of these yield negative Wigner functions, resulting in quasi-probability distributions~\cite{Ferrie_2008,Ferrie_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Following Wigner's work, this line of research has proven to be highly insightful, as the negativity of the Wigner function is regarded as an indicator of quantumness~\cite{Veitch_2012 , preventing the efficient classical simulation of continuous variable states~\cite{Mari_2012 and enabling the onset of quantum advantage~\cite{Gard_2015,doi:10.1126/science.abe8770,Lund_2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: These processes of mapping classical states to quantum states and vice-versa are based on a pair of Quantizer and Dequantizer operators, which we will describe in detail below.
elements.sent-2411-031: The Weyl map associates, for example, to the classical function $pq$ the operator $\frac12(\hat p \hat q +\hat q \hat p)$.
elements.sent-2411-032: This is not the only quantization map possible, for example one can choose to put all the $\hat p$'s on the left.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, this is not the end of the story.
elements.sent-2411-034: Focusing on the creation and annihilation operators Cahill and Glauber~\cite{Cahill:1969it , in the context of their studies on quantization and coherent states, introduced alternative quantizations, using the isomorphism between phase space and the complex plane, parametrized by an asymmetry parameter $s$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Depending on the value of $s$, different orderings are achieved: for $s=0$ we have the symmetric ordering, while for $s=1$ (resp.~$s=-1$) we have the normal (resp.\ antinormal) ordering~\cite{PhysRevA.90.013810,ivan2012measure,tan2020negativity, de2019measuring, berra2020coherent, berra2020star .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The aim of this paper is to investigate in which cases the quantization of the classical Gaussian state~\eqref{Gaussianpq gives rise to a viable quantum density matrix, and to do this for arbitrary ordering of the operators $\hat p, \hat q$ or the creation and annihilation operators.}
elements.sent-2411-037: For a review of quantum phase space distributions one can see~\cite{Lee1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: The issue may also be relevant for the quantization of gravity giving rise to a quantum spacetime and a noncommutative geometry~\cite{Kempf:1994su, Brandenberger:2002nq, Chamseddine_2010, Devastato:2019grb, Huggett:2020kok .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The issue in this case is asking in which case we can have a viable localised state when quantizing spacetime and it leads to the locality of the theory.
elements.sent-2411-040: We work in natural units where $\hbar=1$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Since we are interested in finding a correspondence between a classical state, which is a Gaussian, and a quantum state, let us briefly introduce the concept of Gaussian states~\cite{WANG_2007,Weedbrook_2012,adesso2014continuous,genoni2016conditional in quantum mechanics.}
elements.sent-2411-042: These are an interesting class of quantum states, as they can be efficiently classically simulated~\cite{Mari_2012 and learned~\cite{mele2024learningquantumstatescontinuous .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: As the name suggests, analogous to classical Gaussian distributions, these are density operators uniquely characterized by their first and second moments.
elements.sent-2411-044: Further properties of Gaussian states have been included in the appendix~\cref{Gaussian , as they provide useful context for understanding certain comments made in the main discussion, but do not represent the core focus of the primary analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-045: For the reminder, it is useful to define $p$ and $q$ on a quantum plane defining \be z\coloneqq \frac{1 {\sqrt{2 ({q + i{p )\ ; \ \bar z\coloneqq\frac{1 {\sqrt{2 ({q - i{p ) \ee and their quantum counterpart, the creation and annihilation operators \be a\coloneqq \frac{1 {\sqrt{2 (\hat{q + i\hat{p ) \ ; \ a^{\dag}\coloneq\frac{1 {\sqrt{2 (\hat{q - i\hat{p ) \ee operators (where we omit the hat on $a,a^\dag$ to lighten notations).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We also introduce the Fock basis $\{\ket{k}\}_{k=0 ^{\infty}$, i.e.\ eigenvectors of the photon number operator $\hat{N =a^{\dag a$ with eigenvalue $\hat{N}\ket{k =k\ket{k}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Let us introduce the concepts of quantization map, quantizer, and dequantizer.
elements.sent-2411-048: Since we use the complex coordinate $z$ in the most significant part of this work (the Cahill-Glauber quantization), we will introduce these quantities using this coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-049: For the two maps to be one the inverse of the other it must be: \be \Tr\hat D(z)\hat U(z')=\delta(z-z') \ee We also require that the map is an isometry, in the sense that an $L^1$ function it associated a trace class operator, whose trace equals the integral of the original functions.
elements.sent-2411-050: Given the inherent noncommutativity of position and momentum operators in quantum mechanics, it is necessary to find a way to map the commutative product $qp$ onto the corresponding operators $\hat{q ,\hat{p}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This mapping will henceforth be referred to as a quantization map.
elements.sent-2411-052: The issue is further complicated by the fact that noncommutativity introduces ambiguity in the ordering of these operators, and multiple orderings are possible.
elements.sent-2411-053: Therefore, we begin by considering an arbitrary ordering of $q$ and $p$ using the following quantization map: \be pq\mapsto \frac{1+\gamma {2}\hat{q}\hat{p +\frac{1-\gamma {2}\hat{p}\hat{q \ee with $-1\le \gamma\le 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: More rigorously, following Weyl~\cite{Weyl, Moyal, Groenewold:1946kp, Curtright:2011vw , we can introduce the quantization map $\hat{\Omega _{\gamma}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The Weyl map $\hat{\Omega _{\gamma (\cdot)\,:L_2(\mathbb{R ^{2 )\rightarrow \mathcal{B (\mathcal{H )$, is defined as \be \hat{\Omega _{\gamma (f)=\int\de x\de y \tilde{f (x,y)e^{-i(x\hat{q +y\hat{p ) e^{\frac{i {2}\gamma xy \ee where $\tilde{f (x,y)$ is the Fourier transform of $f(q,p)\in L_{2 (\mathbb{R ^2)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It is easy to show that the action of the map $\hat{\Omega _{\gamma}$ returns the ordering in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ordering .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Notice that in~\eqref{weyl quantization only for the symmetric ordering , i.e.\ $(\gamma=0)$, we have an Hermitian operator, which is one of the necessary conditions to have a quantum state, as the expectation values of Hermitian operators must be real.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Consider the adjoint of expression \eqref{weyl quantization and making the substitution $\xi \rightarrow -\xi$ and $\eta \rightarrow -\eta$.. \begin{equation}\hat{\Omega}^{\dagger}_{\gamma}(f) = \int \de{(-\xi)} \de{(-\eta)} \tilde{f^{*}}(-\xi, -\eta) e^{-i(\xi\hat{q} + \eta\hat{p})} e^{\frac{-1}{2}(i\gamma\xi\eta)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Given that $f(q,p)$ is real, we have $\tilde{f^{* (-\xi,-\eta)=\tilde{f (\xi,\eta)$ (in this case, we used "*" to describe the complex conjugate, to lighten the notation) known as the reality condition.}}}

elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore for the reminder of the section we make exclusively use of the symmetric ordering and denote $\hat{\Omega (\cdot)$ the quantizer corresponding to $\gamma=0$.}

elements.sent-2411-134: This suggests that the ordering of the creation and annihilation operators fundamentally determines whether a valid quantum state can be obtained, regardless of how localized the classical probability density is.
elements.sent-2411-135: Interestingly, the antinormal ordering consistently yields a valid positive quantum state, implying a hierarchy of orderings that is dictated by the physicality of the operators.
elements.sent-2411-136: First, we explored the effects of implementing an arbitrary ordering of position and momentum operators.
elements.sent-2411-137: In this case, we obtain a Hermitian operator only with symmetric ordering, which imposes a constraint on the possible values of $\gamma$, determining the specific ordering of $\hat{q}$ and $\hat{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Additionally, we found that for a valid quantum state, a particle cannot be too sharply localized.
elements.sent-2411-139: More precisely, we identify a critical value $\lambda_c = 1$, where $(2\lambda)^{-1}$ represents the squared variance of the Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-140: A quantum state is possible only for $\lambda \geq \lambda_c$.
elements.sent-2411-141: This implies, for example, that we cannot map a $\delta$-function to a valid quantum state with symmetric ordering.
elements.sent-2411-142: This result aligns with the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, which is satisfied only for states with $\lambda \geq \lambda_c$.
elements.sent-2411-143: The central point of the present work has been the case of an arbitrary ordering of the annihilation and creation operators.
elements.sent-2411-144: In this situation we always find an hermitian operator, hence we do not have a limitation on the values for the parameter $s$.
elements.sent-2411-145: Similarly to the previous case, we find a critical value, actually a critical line $\lambda_c$, which depends on $s$.
elements.sent-2411-146: This critical line is such that only for $\lambda \geq \lambda_c$ we get a quantum state, as shown by~\cref{fig:enter-label .}
elements.sent-2411-147: The most interesting result is found for the antinormal ordering, i.e.\ $s=-1$, corresponding to a critical value $\lambda_c$ which diverges.
elements.sent-2411-148: In this situation we find that even when the variance approaches zero, indicating a particle that is perfectly localized in phase space, the outcome remains a valid operator within the Hilbert space.
elements.sent-2411-149: This means that even sharply peaked classical probability densities can correspond to valid quantum states.

elements.sent-2411-154: Here we introduce some properties of Gaussian states used in the main text.
elements.sent-2411-155: Let us define the operator-valued vector $\hat{R =(\hat{q ,\hat{p )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: The first moment of $\rho$ is defined as $m(\rho)\coloneqq\tr (\hat{R}\rho )$, and the covariance matrix of $\rho$ is defined as \begin{equation}V(\rho)\coloneqq \tr(\{(\hat{R}-m(\rho)),(\hat{R}-m(\rho))^{T}\})\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-157: The covariance matrix $V(\rho)$ is a symplectic matrix, satisfying $V(\rho)\Omega V^{T (\rho)=\Omega$, where $\Omega$ is the usual symplectic form, and it also satisfies \be V(\rho)+i\Omega\geq 0\,.}
elements.sent-2411-158: The converse also holds: given a valid covariance matrix and a vector $m \in \mathbb{R ^2$, there is a unique Gaussian state associated with them.}
elements.sent-2411-159: In particular, a symplectic matrix is a valid covariance matrix if it fulfills Eq.~\eqref{eq:positivity .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Given this result, it is natural to define the Gaussification $G(\rho)$ of a given state $\rho$, that is, the Gaussian state $G(\rho)$ identified by the first moment $m(\rho)$ and the covariance matrix $V(\rho)$ corresponding to $\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-161: To determine the quantization of the Gaussian~\eqref{Gaussianpq , we need to evaluate: \be \rho_{nm =\bra n\hat\Omega(f)\ket m=\int \de x \de y \bra n e^{-i y \hat{q -i x \hat{p }\tilde \rho_\lambda(x,y) \ket m \ee where \be \tilde\rho_\lambda(x,y)=\frac1{2\pi e^{-\frac{x^2+y^2 {4\lambda \ee is the Fourier transform of~\eqref{Gaussianpq .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: To perform the calculation~\eqref{Omega0 , it is useful to express $\hat{q}$ and $\hat{p}$ in terms of the creation and annihalation operators.}
elements.sent-2411-163: We now leverage the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff formula: \be e^{a^\dagger \bar t-a t =e^{a^\dagger \bar t e^{- a t e^{\frac{1 {2 [a^\dagger \bar t, a t] =e^{-\frac{1 {2 |t|^2 e^{a^\dagger \bar t e^{- a t \ee We aim to express the integral in \eqref{Omega0 in terms of the complex variable $t$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: To do so, we use the fact that \be \int \de x \de y= 2 \int \de^2t \ee We can express \eqref{Omega0 as follows: \be \rho_{nm =\bra n\hat\Omega(f)\ket m=\frac{1 {\pi}\int \de^2t \, e^{-\frac{|t|^2 {2\lambda e^{-\frac{1 {2 |t|^2 \bra n e^{a^\dagger \bar t e^{- a t}\ket m \ee This is the same expression we find in Eq.~\eqref{Omegaf for $s=0$, see \ref{App:proofofprop2 for the complete calculation, here let us write the result we are interested in.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: Let us define \be \alpha=\left(\frac{1 2+\frac{1 {2\lambda}\right)=\frac{1+\lambda {2\lambda \ee The result is \be \rho_{nm =\rho_{nn =\frac1\alpha\left(1-\frac1\alpha\right)^n=\frac{2\lambda {1+\lambda}\left(\frac{1-\lambda {1+\lambda}\right)^n \ee Only the diagonal terms survive.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Furthermore, since $\sum_{n=0 ^\infty \left(1-\frac1\alpha\right)^n=\alpha$ we see that also in this case the trace of $\rho=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: To complete the proof we have to analyze what happens for different values of $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-168: The trace of this operator is always 1, but the eigenvalues are all positive only for $\lambda<1$, and likewise $\rho_{00 <1$ for the same range of $\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-169: The case for $\lambda=1$ can be treated separately.
elements.sent-2411-171: In this case the map associates to the Gaussian a pure state: the eigenvector of the number operator corresponding to the zero eigenvalue, (ground state for the harmonic oscillator).
elements.sent-2411-172: Only in this case the Weyl map associates a pure state.
elements.sent-2411-173: For $\lambda<1$ the Weyl maps associates a mixed state.
elements.sent-2411-174: For $\lambda>1$ the map still associates a Hermitean operator, but it cannot be considered a possible quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-175: Let us start with a probability distribution $\rho(q,p)$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Its Weyl-symmetric quantization, i.e.\ $\hat{\Omega (f)$, is diagonal in the Fock basis (see~\cref{App:proofofprop2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-177: This is an important property, as it implies that all terms involving the action of either annihilation or creation operators on $\hat{\Omega (f)$ vanish: \be \tr(a^n \hat{\Omega (f))=\tr((a^\dagger)^n \hat{\Omega (f))=0 \ee This is a remarkable result.}}}
elements.sent-2411-178: In fact, recalling that \be \hat{q =\frac{a + a^\dagger {\sqrt{2 \ , \ \hat{p =\frac{a - a^\dagger {i\sqrt{2 \ , \ \{\hat{q ,\hat{p}\}=\frac{a^2-(a^\dagger)^2 {i \ee we can exploit \eqref{zero , yielding: \ba \tr(\hat{q}\hat{\Omega (f)) = \tr(\hat{p}\hat{\Omega (f))=\tr(\{\hat{q ,\hat{p}\}\hat{\Omega (f))=0 \tr(\hat{q ^2\hat{\Omega (f)) = \tr(\hat{p ^2\hat{\Omega (f)) \ea Define $\Delta\coloneqq\tr(\hat{p ^2\hat{\Omega (f))$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We have that $\hat{\Omega (f)$ has the following covariance matrix \be V(\rho)=\begin{pmatrix \Delta 0 0 \Delta \end{pmatrix \ee Imposing the positivity condition of the Gaussification, i.e.\ Eq.~\eqref{eq:positivity in the main text, we find the condition $\Delta\ge\frac{1 {2}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: We can dequantize the condition and see how it relates to the properties of the classical probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-181: Recalling that we can dequantize symmetric polynomials of $\hat{q ,\hat{p}$, given the symmetric quantization, we have the correspondence $\Delta\mapsto \frac{1 {2 (\Delta_q^2+\Delta_p^2)$, where $\Delta_q,\Delta_p$ are classical variances.}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: We used the fact that the probability distribution is centered in the origin and has expectations of position and momentum zero.
elements.sent-2411-183: The condition $\Delta_q^2+\Delta_p^2\ge 1$ is necessary for the positivity of the quantized probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-184: This is due to the fact that non-symmetric probability distribution in $q,p$ are mapped to symmetric operators in $\hat{q ,\hat{p}$, cfr.\ Eq.~\eqref{eq2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-185: The further requirement that the classical variances are equal to each other $\Delta_q=\Delta p$ recovers $\Delta_q\Delta_p\ge\frac{1 {2}$ as a necessary condition for positivity.}
elements.sent-2411-186: If this is not the case, we can only assert that if $\hat{\Omega (f)$ is non-positive, then necessarily $Delta_q\Delta_p<\frac{1 {2}$ (due to the fact that $\Delta_q\Delta_p\le\frac{1 {2 (\Delta_q^2+\Delta_p^2)$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: Let us introduce the quantizer, which describes arbitrary orderings with respect to the creation and annihilation operators.
elements.sent-2411-188: These types of orderings were first introduced in~\cite{Cahill:1969it (for a more modern analysis, see also~\cite{Manko:2004syv, Lizzi:2014pwa ).}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The quantizer can be expressed as follows: \be \hat D_s(z)=\int \de^2\xi\, e^{\xi(a^\dag -\bar z)-\bar\xi(a-z) e^{\frac{s 2 |\xi|^2 =\int \de^2\xi\, e^{\xi a^\dag e^{-\bar\xi a e^{-\frac{1-s 2 |\xi|^2 e^{-\xi\bar z+\bar\xi z \ee where in the last step we used the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff formula.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The dequantizer exchanges $s\leftrightarrow -s$: \be \hat U_s(z)=\hat D_{-s (z) \ee It is now clear that the quantization map expressed in terms of the Fourier transform of the classic function $f(z)$ is: \be \hat\Omega_s(f)=\int \de^2\xi\, e^{\xi a^\dag e^{-\bar\xi a e^{-\frac{1-s 2 |\xi|^2}\tilde f(\xi) \ee The different orderings depend on $s$, which is why \eqref{incorniciato is referred to as the $s$-ordered map.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: To illustrate the different orderings, let us present the following example: \be \hat\Omega_s(z\bar z)=\frac{1+s 2 a^\dag a + \frac{1-s 2 a a^\dag \ee we see that for $s=0$ we have the symmetric ordering, while for $s=1$ (resp.~$s=-1$) we normal (resp.. antinormal) ordering.}}
elements.sent-2411-192: To determine the quantization of the Gaussian~\eqref{eq:complexgaussian , we need to evaluate: \be \rho_{nm =\bra n\hat\Omega_s(f)\ket m=\int \de^2\xi\,\bra n e^{\xi a^\dagger e^{-\bar\xi a e^{-\frac{1-s 2 |\xi|^2}\tilde \rho_\lambda(\xi) \ket m \ee where \be \tilde\rho_\lambda(\xi)=\frac1{\pi e^{-\frac{|\xi|^2 {2\lambda \ee is the Fourier transform of~\eqref{eq:complexgaussian .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: To calculate $\rho_{nm}$ we need: \be e^{-\bar\xi a}\ket m=\sum_{i=0 ^m (-1)^i\frac{\bar\xi^i {i!}\sqrt{\frac{m! {(m-i)! }\ket{m-i \ee from which we obtain \begin{equation}\bra n e^{\xi a^\dag}e^{-\bar\xi a}\ket m= \sum_{i=0}^m\sum_{j=0}^n \bra{n-j} (-1)^i\frac{\bar\xi^i}{i!}\frac{\xi^j}{j!}\sqrt{\frac{m!}{(m-i)!}} \sqrt{\frac{n!}{(n-j)!}}\ket{m-i}\nonumber = \sum_{i=0}^m\sum_{j=0}^n (-1)^i\frac{\bar\xi^i}{i!}\frac{\xi^j}{j!}\sqrt{\frac{m!}{(m-i)!}} \sqrt{\frac{n!}{(n-j)!}}\delta_{n-j,m-i}\end{equation} Assume for the time being that $n\geq m$ and set $n=m+k$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Then, since $\delta_{n-j,m-i =\delta_{j,k+i}$, we have \be \bra{m+k e^{\xi a^\dag e^{-\bar\xi a}\ket m=\sqrt{m!(m+k)!}\sum_{i=0 ^m \frac{(-1)^i {i! |\xi|^{2i \bar\xi^k \frac1{(i+k)! \frac1{(m-i)! \ee We can immediately anticipate that, setting $\xi=\frac r{\sqrt 2 e^{i\varphi}$, since $\tilde f(\xi)$ depends only on $r$, the angular integration of~\eqref{Omegaf gives a factor of $2\pi$ and imposes $k=0$, We have reached the important result that the operator $\hat\rho$ is diagonal in the discrete basis.}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-209: First we write \be \hat{\Omega _s(f)=\int\de^2z\rho(z)\hat{D _s(z) \ee where \be \hat{D _s\coloneqq \int\de^2\xi e^{-\xi \bar{z +z\bar{\xi e^{\xi a^{\dag e^{-\bar{\xi a e^{-\frac{1-s {2 |\xi|^2 \ee Then express $\Delta$ as \be \Delta=\int\de^2z\rho(z)\tr(\{a,a^{\dag}\}\hat{D _s)= \int\de^2z\rho(z)\tr(\{a,a^{\dag}\}\hat{\Omega ^{-1 _{-s ) \ee where we have identified $\hat{D _s=\hat{\Omega _{-s ^{-1}$, see Section~\ref{app:dequantizer .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
